title: Kotlin Control Flow – Expressions and Pattern Matching

meta.title: Kotlin Control Flow Guide – When, If-Expressions & Loops (2026)
meta.description: Master Kotlin's control flow. Learn about the powerful 'when' expression, if-as-an-expression, and idiomatic looping for native Android development.

content.value: In Kotlin, `if` and `when` are expressions: they can return a value that can be assigned to a variable. As an Architect, you should use this to replace the ternary operator (which Kotlin does not have) and to make your native Android logic more functional and less imperative.
Because `if` returns a value, you can use it directly in assignments. This reduces the scope of variables and makes the intent of your code clearer.

The `when` expression is Kotlin's highly evolved version of the `switch` statement. It is significantly more flexible, allowing you to check types, ranges, and multiple conditions in a single branch. For Revochamp, this is the perfect tool for handling different widget types coming from the AI.
Kotlin simplifies iteration using **Ranges**. You don't write traditional `for (int i=0; i<10; i++)` loops. Instead, you use the `in` operator with ranges like `1..10` or `0 until list.size`.

The `while` and `do-while` loops in Kotlin behave exactly as they do in Dart and Java. Use these when the number of iterations is not known beforehand, such as waiting for a background AI process to finish.

faq.question: Does 'when' require an 'else' branch?
faq.answer: If you use 'when' as an expression (assigning it to a variable), the compiler must be sure all cases are covered. If you are checking an Enum or a Sealed Class, and you cover all cases, 'else' is not needed. Otherwise, 'else' is mandatory.

faq.question: How do I break out of nested loops in Kotlin?
faq.answer: Kotlin supports 'Labels'. You can mark a loop with a label (e.g., 'loop@') and then use 'break@loop' to exit specifically from that outer loop, which is much cleaner than using boolean flags.

faq.question: Can I use 'when' without an argument?
faq.answer: Yes! When used without an argument, it acts as a more readable replacement for an if-else-if chain. Each branch condition must evaluate to a Boolean.

quiz.question: Which Kotlin structure serves as a more powerful replacement for the traditional switch statement?
What is the result of using a range like '1 until 5' in a loop?
True or False: Kotlin does not have a ternary operator (condition ? true : false).
